content:
# Genesis Chapter 32 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 18v–19r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_032_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_032_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 29–30 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0033 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0034

---

**Manuscript Confirmation:** The Leningrad Codex images (Folios 18v–19r, West Semitic Research Project, Bruce E. Zuckerman) have been read directly by all three council translators. Folio 18v (GEN_032_page_001.jpg) is photographed inverted per WSRP archival convention; once re-oriented, it carries the conclusion of Genesis 31 and then the full opening of Genesis 32 — the Mahanaim encounter (vv. 1–2), the dispatch of messengers to Esau (vv. 3–5), the return report and fear (vv. 6–8), Jacob's prayer (vv. 9–12), and the preparation of the gift-caravan through v. 21. Three-column Sephardic square script with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation is legible throughout. Folio 19r (GEN_032_page_002.jpg) is in standard orientation and carries the wrestling narrative from v. 22 through v. 33 and into the opening of Genesis 33. Key readings confirmed by all three translators: יַבֹּק (Jabbok, v. 22), וַיֵּאָבֵק (wrestled, v. 24), כַּף-יְרֵכוֹ (hip socket, v. 25), יִשְׂרָאֵל (Israel, v. 28), פְּנִיאֵל (Peniel, v. 30), פְּנוּאֵל (Penuel, v. 31), גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה (gid ha-nasheh, vv. 32–33), and צֹלֵעַ (limping, v. 31). Codex Vaticanus pages 29–30 carry the Greek LXX in continuous uncial, with CAP. XXXVI beginning on page 29 and CAP. XXXVII beginning on page 30. No Dead Sea Scrolls fragments of Genesis 32 are extant; the Leningrad Codex and Codex Vaticanus are the principal manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.

---

*Jacob Encounters Angels at Mahanaim (vv. 1–2)*

**32:1** Jacob went on his way — and the angels of God met him.

**32:2** When Jacob saw them, he said, "This is the camp of God!" And he called the name of that place Mahanaim.

---

*Jacob Sends Messengers to Esau (vv. 3–5)*

**32:3** And Jacob sent messengers before him to his brother Esau, in the land of Seir, the country of Edom,

**32:4** instructing them: "Thus you shall say to my lord Esau: Thus says your servant Jacob — I have sojourned with Laban and stayed until now.

**32:5** I have oxen and donkeys, flocks, male servants and female servants. I am sending word to tell my lord, that I may find favor in your eyes."

---

*The Messengers Return — Esau Is Coming with Four Hundred Men (vv. 6–8)*

**32:6** And the messengers returned to Jacob, saying, "We came to your brother Esau, and he is coming to meet you — and four hundred men are with him."

**32:7** Then Jacob was greatly afraid and distressed. He divided the people who were with him, and the flocks and the herds and the camels, into two camps,

**32:8** thinking, "If Esau comes to the one camp and strikes it, then the camp that is left will escape."

---

*Jacob's Prayer (vv. 9–12)*

**32:9** And Jacob said, "O God of my father Abraham and God of my father Isaac — O LORD, who said to me, 'Return to your land and to your kindred, and I will do you good' —

**32:10** I am not worthy of the least of all the steadfast love and all the faithfulness that you have shown to your servant. For with only my staff I crossed this Jordan, and now I have become two camps.

**32:11** Please deliver me from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esau, for I fear him — lest he come and strike me, the mothers with the children.

**32:12** But you said, 'I will surely do you good, and make your offspring as the sand of the sea, which cannot be numbered for multitude.'"

---

*The Gift-Caravan Prepared and Sent Before Jacob (vv. 13–21)*

**32:13** So he lodged there that night, and from what he had with him he took a gift for his brother Esau:

**32:14** two hundred female goats and twenty male goats, two hundred ewes and twenty rams,

**32:15** thirty nursing camels and their young, forty cows and ten bulls, twenty female donkeys and ten male donkeys.

**32:16** These he handed over to his servants, each drove by itself, and said to his servants, "Pass on before me and put a space between drove and drove."

**32:17** He instructed the first: "When my brother Esau meets you and asks you, 'To whom do you belong? Where are you going? And whose are these ahead of you?' —

**32:18** then you shall say, 'They belong to your servant Jacob. It is a gift sent to my lord Esau. And behold, he himself is behind us.'"

**32:19** He likewise instructed the second and the third and all who followed the droves: "You shall say the same thing to Esau when you find him.

**32:20** And you shall say, 'Moreover, your servant Jacob is behind us.'" For he thought: I will appease him with the gift going before me, and afterward I will see his face — perhaps he will accept me.

**32:21** So the gift passed on before him, and he himself lodged that night in the camp.

---

*Jacob Crosses the Jabbok — He Sends His Family Over (vv. 22–23)*

**32:22** That same night he arose and took his two wives, his two female servants, and his eleven children, and crossed the ford of the Jabbok.

**32:23** He took them and sent them across the stream, and sent across everything that he had.

---

*The Man Wrestles with Jacob Until Dawn (vv. 24–26)*

**32:24** And Jacob was left alone. And a man wrestled with him until the breaking of the dawn.

**32:25** When the man saw that he did not prevail against him, he touched the socket of his hip — and Jacob's hip was put out of joint as he wrestled with him.

**32:26** Then the man said, "Let me go, for the dawn has broken." But Jacob said, "I will not let you go unless you bless me."

---

*The Name Changed — Israel (vv. 27–29)*

**32:27** And the man said to him, "What is your name?" And he said, "Jacob."

**32:28** Then he said, "Your name shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel — for you have striven with God and with men, and have prevailed."

**32:29** Then Jacob asked him, "Please tell me your name." But he said, "Why is it that you ask my name?" And he blessed him there.

---

*The Naming of Peniel (vv. 30–31)*

**32:30** So Jacob called the name of the place Peniel, saying, "For I have seen God face to face, and yet my life has been delivered."

**32:31** The sun rose upon him as he passed over Penuel, and he was limping because of his hip.

---

*The Dietary Prohibition — The Sinew of the Hip Socket (v. 32)*

**32:32** Therefore the people of Israel do not eat the sinew of the hip socket that is on the socket of the thigh to this day, because he touched the socket of Jacob's hip at the sinew of the hip socket.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### The Architecture of Genesis 32: Three Encounters and a Law

All three council translators identified the same four-movement structure, though they emphasize different aspects of it. LOGOS's formulation is adopted by the council: the chapter moves from a brief angelic encounter at the land's border (vv. 1–2), through a prolonged episode of fear, prayer, and strategy in the face of the Esau threat (vv. 3–21), to the nocturnal wrestling at the Jabbok (vv. 22–31), and closes with the etiological dietary prohibition (v. 32). PNEUMA's observation is also adopted: the structural center of gravity is the single loaded sentence that opens the wrestling pericope — "Jacob was left alone. And a man wrestled with him until the breaking of the dawn" — around which the entire chapter orbits.

EMET identifies the chapter's dominant wordplay with the precision required: the three words יַעֲקֹב (Ya'aqov, "Jacob"), יַבֹּק (Yabboq, "Jabbok"), and וַיֵּאָבֵק (wayyê'āveq, "and he wrestled") share the same root consonants rearranged — name, place, and action collapsing into each other at the ford of the river in the dark. All three translators note this wordplay; PNEUMA provides the most evocative formulation: "The place and the man and the action are all bound together in sound." The council adopts this as a standing observation for the chapter, as it cannot be reproduced in English but must be noted.

LOGOS provides the most systematic structural theology: Genesis 32 is organized around the progressive intensification of Jacob's vulnerability — from the divine witness at Mahanaim, to the anticipated encounter with Esau, to the anonymous nocturnal wrestling that finally marks and renames him. The movement is from heavenly to earthly-feared to mysteriously divine. This framework is adopted by the council.

---

### On 32:1–2 — Mahanaim and the *mal'akhim* Echo ⚠️

**EMET** and **LOGOS** both render v. 1 as "the angels of God met him" and v. 3 as "messengers." **PNEUMA** renders both as "angels" (v. 1) and "messengers" (vv. 3, 6) without explicitly calling attention to the Hebrew identity of the words.

The council confirms the fundamental issue: the Hebrew word מַלְאָכִים (*mal'akhim*) is identical in vv. 1 and 3 — the same word used for the divine beings who meet Jacob is used for the human emissaries Jacob sends to Esau. This is demonstrably intentional: Jacob moves from receiving divine messengers to dispatching human messengers in a single narrative breath. EMET's note on this structural mirroring — heaven sends emissaries to Jacob; Jacob sends emissaries to Esau — is adopted by the council as the definitive observation. The LXX Vaticanus uses *angeloi* (ἄγγελοι) for both, preserving the identical ambiguity.

The consensus translation renders v. 1 as "angels" and vv. 3 and 6 as "messengers," following the contextual distinction while noting the Hebrew identity of the words. This is the standard practice of all three translators and the LXX alike.

On the Mahanaim naming: the place-name מַחֲנַיִם (*Machanayim*) is the dual form of *machaneh* ("camp") — "Two Camps." The council follows all three translators in retaining "Mahanaim" as a proper name without translation, per standard convention, while noting the dual's foreshadowing function: Jacob's declaration "this is the camp of God" (singular *machaneh*) will be echoed when he splits his own household into two camps (plural *machanot*, v. 7), and both echo the Mahanaim naming. The structural doubling is part of the narrative design.

---

### On 32:7 — "Greatly afraid and distressed"

**EMET** renders "greatly afraid and distressed." **LOGOS** renders "greatly afraid and distressed." **PNEUMA** renders "terrified" and "in anguish."

All three translators are agreed on the two distinct emotional states — fear (יִרְאָה, *yir'ah*) and distress (וַיֵּצֶר לוֹ, *wayyetser lo*, literally "it was narrow to him," from the root meaning to be in straits). The council adopts the EMET/LOGOS rendering "greatly afraid and distressed" — two distinct terms, matching the Hebrew's two-term construction. PNEUMA's "terrified" and "in anguish" is vivid but introduces a slight register escalation. The Hebrew's two separate verbs should remain two separate English terms. EMET's additional note — that *tsarar* (to be in straits) anticipates the gorge geography and the wrenching of the hip — is adopted as a council observation.

---

### On 32:10 — *qatonti mikkol hachasadim* — "I am not worthy of the least" ⚠️

This verse represents one of the most significant and carefully considered translation decisions in the chapter.

**EMET** renders: "I am too small for all the steadfast-loves and from all the faithfulness" — preserving the literal force of *qatonti* ("I have become too small") with the plural *chasadim* ("steadfast-loves").

**LOGOS** renders: "I am not worthy of the least of all the steadfast love and all the faithfulness" — a formal rendering that captures the superlative force of *qatonti mikkol* ("too small for all") while reading naturally in English.

**PNEUMA** renders: "I am not worthy of the least of all the faithful love and all the faithfulness" — essentially identical to LOGOS in sense, with "faithful love" substituting for "steadfast love."

The council deliberates on two issues:

**First issue — the verbal rendering of *qatonti*:** EMET's "I am too small" is the more literal rendering; LOGOS/PNEUMA's "I am not worthy of the least" captures the comparative force more naturally in English. The council adopts "I am not worthy of the least of all the steadfast love" (LOGOS). The comparative construction *qatonti mikkol* — "too small for [all of] this" — expresses genuine unworthiness: Jacob is not large enough to contain or deserve what has been given him. The LOGOS/PNEUMA rendering preserves this force.

**Second issue — *chasadim* (plural) vs. "steadfast love" (singular):** EMET's "steadfast-loves" (plural) honors the Hebrew plural but is unusual in English. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "steadfast love" is the standard formal equivalent. The council adopts "steadfast love" (singular rendering of the plural) — the plural in Hebrew intensifies and enumerates rather than requiring a plural noun in English.

**Third issue — LXX variant:** All three translators note the LXX Vaticanus divergence: where the MT reads *qatonti mikkol hachasadim* ("I am too small for all the steadfast loves"), the LXX reads *hikanoumai en pasē dikaiosynē* ("I am sufficient in all righteousness" — Vaticanus) or *hikanei moi* ("it is sufficient for me" — EMET's reading). EMET reads this as opposite in sense to the MT — a confession of adequacy rather than inadequacy. PNEUMA reads the Vaticanus variant more charitably: "I am sufficient" could be read as "I am overwhelmed with sufficiency — I have been given more than enough." The council notes the divergence but follows the MT, which is the more internally coherent reading in context: Jacob's prayer is a humble petition for deliverance, not a declaration of satisfaction. The LXX variant is documented in the Disagreement Log.

The council also confirms EMET's reading of the second half of the verse: "with only my staff I crossed this Jordan, and now I have become two camps" — the Jordan (הַיַּרְדֵּן הַזֶּה, "this Jordan") is the specific border Jacob crossed when he fled to Laban with nothing, and the two camps echo the Mahanaim naming. The structural irony is deliberate: he crossed with a stick; he returns with two organized camps. All three translators preserve this contrast.

---

### On 32:20 — *'akhapperah fanaw* — "Appease" and the Atonement Root ⚠️

**EMET** renders: "Let me appease his face with the gift" — preserving *'akhapperah* (from *kipper*, the atonement root) with the note that this is the same root as *kapporet* (the mercy seat) and Yom Kippur.

**LOGOS** renders: "I may appease him with the present" — standard formal rendering, with a note flagging the atonement vocabulary.

**PNEUMA** renders: "I will pacify him with the gift" — dynamic rendering, with a substantial note on the *kapar* root and its Day of Atonement resonance.

The council adopts "appease" (EMET, LOGOS) rather than "pacify" (PNEUMA). The verb *kipper* in the Pi'el — "to cover over, to make atonement, to pacify" — is rendered "appease" by the two more source-oriented translators, and this is the standard rendering in formal English translations. "Pacify" is a reasonable dynamic equivalent but loses the specific cultic register of *kipper*.

The council adopts EMET and LOGOS on the form: "I will appease him with the gift going before me." The council further adopts — from all three translators — the explicit note that *'akhapperah* is the atonement root, the same root underlying *kapporet* ("mercy seat") and *Yom Kippur* ("Day of Atonement"). The LXX Vaticanus reads *hilaskomai* (to propitiate, to make atonement) — a cultic term that preserves the atoning force of *kipper* exactly. This verbal echo across the cultic-narrative boundary is one of the most theologically rich moments in the chapter.

The full formula of v. 20 — "perhaps he will accept me" — requires a note. LOGOS renders the final clause "Perhaps he will accept me." EMET renders "perhaps he will lift up my face" — preserving the idiom שָׂא פָנִים (literally "lift up the face," the idiom for showing favor). PNEUMA renders "maybe he will accept me." The council adopts "perhaps he will accept me" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) as the natural English equivalent of the idiom. EMET's literal rendering "lift up my face" is noted as the underlying Hebrew idiom.

---

### On 32:24 — "A man wrestled with him" — The Identity of the *'ish* ⚠️

**EMET** renders: "a man wrestled with him" — declining to identify the *'ish*.

**LOGOS** renders: "a man wrestled with him" — same deliberate ambiguity.

**PNEUMA** renders: "a man wrestled with him" — same.

There is complete unanimity on the translation. The council confirms this as the only legitimate rendering: אִישׁ (*'ish*) means "a man," and the text's deliberate reticence about this being's identity is itself the central theological gesture. The four interpretive options — angel, God himself, the divine guardian of Esau, Jacob's own inner self — are documented in EMET's textual notes and acknowledged by all three translators. The council adopts EMET's formulation as definitive: "the ambiguity is the text's own and must not be smoothed away by editorial choice."

PNEUMA's note is adopted as the council's theological summary: "Jacob does not know what he is wrestling with until it is over. He fights in the dark." The liminal darkness, the physical grappling, and the dawn breaking at the moment of resolution are all part of the text's deliberate structuring of the encounter as irreducibly mysterious.

The LXX Vaticanus reads *anthrōpos* ("a man/person"), consistent with the MT. No ancient witness glosses the identity.

---

### On 32:25 — *kaf-yereko* — "Hip socket" vs. "Thigh socket" ⚠️

**EMET** renders: "the socket of his thigh" — anatomically, translating *kaf-yarekh* as hip socket throughout.

**LOGOS** renders: "the socket of his hip" — anatomically precise.

**PNEUMA** renders: "his hip socket" — same sense.

There is effective agreement on substance. *כַּף-יָרֵךְ* (*kaf-yarekh*) — hollow/socket (*kaf*) + thigh/hip (*yarekh*) — designates the acetabulum, the cup-shaped socket of the hip joint. The disagreement is terminological: "socket of his thigh" (EMET) vs. "socket of his hip" (LOGOS, PNEUMA). The council adopts "the socket of his hip" (LOGOS, PNEUMA). While *yarekh* can mean "thigh" in other contexts (and EMET is technically accurate to that primary sense), the anatomical structure being dislocated is the hip socket, and "hip socket" is the more precise and immediately recognizable term in English. The LXX Vaticanus reads *to platos tou mērou* ("the breadth of his thigh") — anatomically less specific than the MT.

The council adopts "hip socket" consistently through vv. 25, 31, and 32.

---

### On 32:28 — The Name Israel — Etymology and Ambiguity ⚠️

**EMET** renders: "for you have striven with God and with men and have prevailed" — using "striven" for שָׂרִיתָ (*sarita*), from the root *sarah* (to strive, contend).

**LOGOS** renders: "for you have striven with God and with men, and have prevailed" — identical.

**PNEUMA** renders: "because you have wrestled with God and with human beings, and you have prevailed" — substituting "wrestled" for "striven."

The council adopts "striven" (EMET, LOGOS) rather than "wrestled" (PNEUMA). The verb שָׂרָה (*sarah*) means to strive, to contend, to persist, to struggle — it is the specific root from which the name Israel (יִשְׂרָאֵל, *Yisra'el*) derives its etymology. Using "wrestled" (the verb of the narrative action) conflates the general etymology with the specific combat of this night; "striven" preserves the broader meaning that Jacob has contended not only in the Jabbok wrestling but throughout his life — with Esau, with Laban, with fate, with God. The council adopts EMET and LOGOS.

On the name's ambiguity: all three translators note that *Yisra'el* = *sarah* + *El* can be read as "he who strives with God" (human as subject) or "God strives" (God as subject). PNEUMA's formulation is adopted by the council as the most elegant statement of the deliberate double meaning: "Whether the name means 'God strives' or 'he who strives with God' is itself left deliberately ambiguous: both readings are in the word." The divine being gives Jacob a name but will not give his own name — this asymmetry is the chapter's supreme irony.

The LXX Vaticanus renders *ēnischysas meta Theou kai meta anthrōpōn dynatos* ("you were strong with God and with men, being powerful") — using *ēnischysas* (from *ischys*, "strength") rather than a striving/contending verb. All three translators note this variant; the council follows the MT's striving/prevailing dynamic.

---

### On 32:29 — The Divine Name Withheld

**EMET** renders: "Why is this — you ask my name?" preserving the interrogative closely.

**LOGOS** renders: "Why is it that you ask my name?" — natural English preserving the *lamah zeh* construction.

**PNEUMA** renders: "Why are you asking for my name?" — more dynamic.

The council adopts "Why is it that you ask my name?" (LOGOS). The demonstrative זֶּה (*zeh*, "this") — *lamah zeh tish'al lishmi* — adds a note of mild rebuke and wonder: "Why — this thing — would you ask for my name?" LOGOS's rendering captures this best without being awkward. PNEUMA drops the *zeh* emphasis. EMET's rendering is clipped but faithful.

All three translators observe the parallel with Judges 13:17–18 (Manoah's identical question to the angel, who refuses: "Why do you ask my name? It is *peli'i* [wonderful/too wonderful]"). The council adopts this as a standing note: the pattern of divine name-withholding is a consistent feature of theophanies in the Hebrew Bible, establishing the asymmetry of the divine-human encounter. You cannot command what you cannot name.

---

### On 32:30–31 — Peniel / Penuel ⚠️

**EMET** renders: "Peniel" (v. 30) / "Penuel" (v. 31) — following the MT's two spellings exactly.

**LOGOS** renders: "Peniel" (v. 30) / "Penuel" (v. 31) — same.

**PNEUMA** renders: "Peniel" (v. 30) / "Penuel" (v. 31) — same.

There is complete unanimity on preserving both spellings. All three translators note that פְּנִיאֵל (*Pĕni'el*, v. 30) is Jacob's theophanic naming — the theological moment — while פְּנוּאֵל (*Pĕnu'el*, v. 31) is the narrator's use of the place-name as a geographical toponym. Both mean "Face of God." The Leningrad Codex clearly has two different spellings in adjacent verses; EMET and PNEUMA both confirm visual confirmation of this on Folio 19r. The LXX Vaticanus uses a single form (Φανουήλ, *Phanouel*) for both verses.

The council adopts the two spellings — Peniel / Penuel — as the MT has them, preserving the distinction between the theological naming and the geographical reference.

On Jacob's declaration "I have seen God face to face, and yet my life has been delivered": all three translators agree. The verb נִצַּל (*nittsal*, Niphal) — "was delivered, was snatched away" — conveys the astonishment of survival; EMET's note is adopted: Jacob expected death and instead received blessing. The LXX *esōthē* ("was saved") shifts slightly toward a positive salvation term from the MT's more raw "was snatched out."

---

### On 32:31 — "The sun rose upon him" and the limping

All three translators preserve "the sun rose upon him" (preserving the *lamed* of advantage: the sun rose *for him*, *upon him*, specifically). The council adopts this phrasing with the dative note: PNEUMA's observation — "not just 'the sun rose' but it rose *for him*, as if it is a personal dawn" — is the most evocative formulation and is adopted as the council's theological note. The LXX Vaticanus reads *anateílantos de autō tou hēliou* ("the sun having risen for him"), preserving the dative.

The participle צֹלֵעַ (*tsolea'*, "limping") is unanimously rendered "limping" across all three translators. The council adopts this.

---

### On 32:32 — *gid ha-nasheh* — The Sinew of the Hip Socket ⚠️

**EMET** renders: "the sinew of the hip-socket" — transliterating *gid ha-nasheh* in the notes but rendering anatomically in the text.

**LOGOS** renders: "the sinew of the thigh" — following the standard formal English convention (KJV/ESV/NASB).

**PNEUMA** renders: "the tendon attached to the hip socket" — anatomically descriptive and dynamic.

The council faces a genuine disagreement on the rendering of גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה (*gid ha-nasheh*):

- גִּיד (*gid*) = sinew, tendon, nerve — not disputed
- הַנָּשֶׁה (*ha-nasheh*) = from נָשָׁה (*nashah*, "to forget/be displaced/slip away") — the "displaced sinew," the sciatic nerve or hip tendon

LOGOS's "sinew of the thigh" follows the traditional formal English rendering (KJV, ESV, NASB) and is the most immediately recognizable to readers of those traditions. EMET's "sinew of the hip-socket" is anatomically more precise, connecting *gid ha-nasheh* to *kaf-yarekh* (hip socket) that appears in the same verse and in vv. 25 and 31. PNEUMA's "tendon attached to the hip socket" is the most anatomically explicit but slightly paraphrastic.

The council adopts "the sinew of the hip socket" (following EMET), for two reasons: (1) it aligns with the consistent use of "hip socket" (*kaf-yarekh*) throughout the chapter and connects the dietary law explicitly to the injury in v. 25; (2) "sinew of the thigh" (LOGOS) is the more traditional English rendering but tends to be read as referring simply to the thigh muscle, losing the connection to the specific joint injury. The compound rendering "sinew of the hip socket" echoes the anatomical terminology of the wrestling narrative and makes the etiological link clear.

The LXX Vaticanus reads *to neuron to platos tou mērou* ("the sinew of the breadth of the thigh") — a general anatomical description rather than a specific technical term. PNEUMA also notes the LXX Vaticanus variant *to neuron ho enarkēsen* ("the sinew that went numb/shrank") — a vivid reading that PNEUMA describes as "the sinew that went numb." EMET notes this term does not appear in the Vaticanus page directly but may reflect a variant in the LXX tradition. The council adopts EMET's caution and notes the LXX variant as tradition-dependent.

On the dietary prohibition itself: the council adopts LOGOS's formulation as the fullest theological note — verse 32 is the first time בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (*benei Yisra'el*, "the people of Israel") appears as a collective designation in Genesis, the moment when the individual patriarch's wound becomes the community's embodied practice. The law is inscribed not in text but in the body — in what is not eaten. Every act of refraining is a commemoration of the Jabbok night. This is adopted as the council's closing theological observation for the chapter.

---

### Summary of SOPHIA's Consensus Decisions

| Verse | Decision | From |
|-------|----------|------|
| 32:1 | "Angels" (v.1) / "messengers" (v.3/6) — same Hebrew word, contextual distinction | All three (confirmed) |
| 32:2 | "Mahanaim" as proper name; exclamation "This is the camp of God!" | All three |
| 32:10 | "I am not worthy of the least of all the steadfast love" | LOGOS / PNEUMA |
| 32:20 | "I will appease him with the gift" — atonement root noted | EMET / LOGOS |
| 32:24 | "A man wrestled with him" — identity deliberately unresolved | All three (unanimous) |
| 32:25 | "The socket of his hip" | LOGOS / PNEUMA |
| 32:28 | "You have striven with God and with men, and have prevailed" | EMET / LOGOS |
| 32:30–31 | "Peniel" (v.30) / "Penuel" (v.31) — two spellings preserved | All three (unanimous) |
| 32:32 | "The sinew of the hip socket" | EMET (modified) |

---

## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### ⚠️ 32:1–2 — The *mal'akhim* Double Use

**Issue:** The Hebrew word מַלְאָכִים (*mal'akhim*) appears in both v. 1 (the divine beings meeting Jacob) and v. 3 (the human emissaries Jacob dispatches). It is the same word. All three translators acknowledge this wordplay but vary in how much weight they give it.

**EMET's position:** The double use is not accidental but structurally deliberate — a mirroring of the divine and human realms. Renders "angels" (v. 1) and "messengers" (v. 3).

**LOGOS's position:** Notes the identical Hebrew but follows standard convention: "angels" (v. 1), "messengers" (v. 3). Structural note in translation notes.

**PNEUMA's position:** Notes the double use but gives it less explicit attention in the translation text itself. Renders identically to EMET and LOGOS.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council translates contextually (angels/messengers) while affirming EMET's structural observation as definitive. The wordplay is non-trivial: the narrative places Jacob in the position of imitating the divine pattern — he receives *mal'akhim* from God and immediately dispatches *mal'akhim* to Esau. This is the closest the chapter comes to showing Jacob as a figure working both the divine and human registers simultaneously, before the wrestling strips that calculation away.

---

### ⚠️ 32:10 — *qatonti mikkol hachasadim* — "I am not worthy" / MT vs. LXX

**Issue:** MT reads *qatonti mikkol hachasadim* — "I am too small for all the steadfast loves" — a confession of unworthiness. LXX Vaticanus reads *hikanoumai en pasē dikaiosynē* ("I am sufficient in all righteousness") or *hikanei moi* ("it is sufficient for me") — which EMET reads as the opposite sense.

**EMET's position:** The LXX is "opposite in sense" from the MT's confession of inadequacy. Follows the MT as contextually superior.

**LOGOS's position:** LXX "appears to follow a different Vorlage or different reading" from the MT. Follows the MT as the more internally coherent reading given the penitential tone of the prayer.

**PNEUMA's position:** The Vaticanus reading *hikanós eimi* can be read charitably — "I am sufficient" meaning "I have been given more than enough" — as a positive-framing variant of the same humility. Notes the LXX variant without resolving it.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council follows the MT. PNEUMA's generous reading of the LXX is noted but does not change the council's judgment. The MT's *qatonti* — "I have become too small" — is a well-established expression of unworthiness (cf. 2 Samuel 7:18, "Who am I, O Lord GOD?"). The prayer's entire structure — humble address, promise recall, petition for deliverance — coheres with the MT's confession of inadequacy, not the LXX's sufficiency. The LXX may reflect a different Vorlage, a copyist's misreading of the root *qaton*, or a theological softening. The council documents the variant but translates the MT.

---

### ⚠️ 32:20 — *'akhapperah fanaw* — "Appease" and the Atonement Root

**Issue:** The verb אֲכַפְּרָה (*'akhapperah*) is the Pi'el cohortative of כָּפַר (*kapar*) — the root underlying *kapporet* (mercy seat) and *Yom Kippur* (Day of Atonement). The question is whether the consensus translation should mark this theological resonance in the text or reserve it to the notes.

**EMET's position:** Renders "Let me appease his face" — uses the face/face idiom — and flags the atonement root prominently in the textual notes.

**LOGOS's position:** Renders "I may appease him" and notes the atonement vocabulary in the structural notes section.

**PNEUMA's position:** Renders "I will pacify him" and devotes an extended note to the *kapar* root and its Day of Atonement resonance.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Appease" is adopted over "pacify" — "appease" carries a slightly more formal, quasi-judicial register that fits the cultic weight of *kipper* better than "pacify." The consensus text does not render *'akhapperah* as "make atonement" (which would over-determine the theology and anachronistically project the Levitical system onto the Jacob narrative), but the council's notes make the atonement connection explicit. The LXX's *hilaskomai* ("propitiate, make atonement") supports the atoning force of the verb. The resonance between Jacob's gift-sending and the later sacrificial system — both described with the language of *kipper* — is one of the chapter's most theologically productive moments and is documented in the council notes.

---

### ⚠️ 32:24 — The Identity of the *'ish* ("A Man")

**Issue:** The being who wrestles with Jacob is called simply אִישׁ (*'ish*, "a man"). Jacob calls the site "Face of God" (v. 30). The wrestler gives Jacob the name "Israel" ("he who strived with God"). The wrestler will not reveal his own name. The interpretive tradition is divided across four main positions (angel, God himself, divine guardian of Esau, inner/allegorical reading).

**All three translators agree:** Render "a man" without identification. The ambiguity is the text's own. No ancient witness — not the MT, not the LXX Vaticanus, not Hosea 12:4 — fully resolves it.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council is unanimous: "a man" is the only legitimate rendering. The text has left the identification open deliberately. The three council translators contribute complementary observations that the council adopts together:
- EMET: documents the four-position interpretive history comprehensively
- LOGOS: notes the narrative logic — "the being whose power is limited by the dawn, who knows Jacob's name, who renames him, who refuses his own name" — that points toward the divine
- PNEUMA: provides the existential-theological reading — Jacob fights in the dark and does not know what he is wrestling with until it is over

The council adopts all three contributions as layers of valid insight without collapsing the ambiguity into a verdict.

---

### ⚠️ 32:25 — *kaf-yereko* — "Socket of His Hip" vs. "Socket of His Thigh"

**Issue:** EMET renders "socket of his thigh" (following *yarekh* = thigh). LOGOS and PNEUMA render "socket of his hip" (following the anatomical identity of the structure).

**EMET's position:** *yarekh* primarily means "thigh" — "thigh socket" is the literal rendering. The hip-socket identification is anatomical inference.

**LOGOS's position:** *kaf-yarekh* designates the hip socket anatomically — "socket of his hip" is both accurate and more immediately communicative to the English reader.

**PNEUMA's position:** "Hip socket" — agrees with LOGOS on anatomical grounds.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "socket of his hip" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) — 2 against 1. The anatomical structure being dislocated is the hip socket (acetabulum), and "socket of his hip" communicates this immediately. "Socket of his thigh" is technically accurate to the primary lexical meaning of *yarekh* but creates a slightly awkward English phrase and may mislead readers into thinking of a knee or femoral joint rather than the hip. EMET's literalism is acknowledged but yielded in favor of anatomical precision and English clarity.

---

### ⚠️ 32:28 — Israel — "Striven" vs. "Wrestled"

**Issue:** EMET and LOGOS render *sarita* as "striven." PNEUMA renders it as "wrestled."

**EMET's position:** שָׂרָה (*sarah*) = to strive, contend, persist — the broader root from which Israel's name is derived. "Striven" preserves the etymological scope: Jacob has striven not only in this wrestling match but throughout his life.

**LOGOS's position:** "Striven" captures the broader contending/struggling dynamic of *sarah* better than "wrestled," which is too tied to the specific physical encounter.

**PNEUMA's position:** "Wrestled" connects the etymology to the immediate action, making the name-giving feel more viscerally connected to the moment.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "striven" (EMET, LOGOS) — 2 against 1. The name Israel is etymologically derived from *sarah*, which has a broader semantic range than wrestling alone. The 'ish himself says "you have striven with God and with men" — the plural "men" indicates the explanation covers Jacob's whole biography, not only this one night. "Wrestled" would narrow that scope inappropriately. "Striven" preserves the full weight of the etymology.

---

### ⚠️ 32:32 — *gid ha-nasheh* — "Sinew of the Hip Socket" vs. "Sinew of the Thigh"

**Issue:** LOGOS renders "the sinew of the thigh" (traditional formal English rendering, following KJV/ESV/NASB). EMET renders "the sinew of the hip-socket" (anatomically connecting to *kaf-yarekh*). PNEUMA renders "the tendon attached to the hip socket" (paraphrastic but anatomically explicit).

**EMET's position:** "Sinew of the hip-socket" is more precise and connects the dietary law to the injury in v. 25. *gid ha-nasheh* = the sciatic nerve / hip tendon — the structure adjacent to *kaf-yarekh*.

**LOGOS's position:** "Sinew of the thigh" follows all the standard formal English translations (KJV, ESV, NASB) and is the most widely recognized rendering. Changing it may confuse readers expecting the traditional term.

**PNEUMA's position:** "Tendon attached to the hip socket" is anatomically accurate and makes the narrative connection explicit, but is slightly paraphrastic.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "sinew of the hip socket" (EMET's sense, modified from PNEUMA's formulation). The reasoning: (1) the dietary prohibition is explicitly and immediately etiological — it exists because of the *kaf-yarekh* dislocation in v. 25. Using "hip socket" consistently across vv. 25, 31, and 32 makes this etiological logic textually visible. (2) "Sinew of the thigh" (LOGOS) risks losing the specific connection to the joint injury that grounds the prohibition. (3) PNEUMA's "tendon attached to the hip socket" is accurate but three words where two will do. The council's compromise — "sinew of the hip socket" — preserves EMET's anatomical precision, aligns with PNEUMA's sense, and is not significantly longer than the traditional rendering.

LOGOS's appeal to traditional English convention is noted and respected. Readers coming from ESV/NASB/KJV traditions will recognize "sinew of the thigh" as a familiar phrase. The Disagreement Log documents this decision for any future editorial review.
